data.title: The Deficit Is Exploding. Some Radical Steps to Rein It In. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Randall W. Forsyth

Little wonder things like the federal budget were obscured this past week in the swirl of events in the Middle East. But the biggest loser in the Iran conflict likely will be the U.S. deficit.

That observation comes from Libby Cantrill, Pimco's keen Washington watcher, who outlined in a recent client note the government's latest funding requests. Not only has the administration of President Donald Trump requested a "supplemental" $200 billion in additional defense spending to replenish munitions and provide more funding for the Iran war in the current fiscal year, she noted, but its recently released outline for next year asks for a stunning increase in defense expenditures, to $1.5 trillion from $1.1 trillion.

The administration's outline of the fiscal 2027 budget also brings back an old friend from the 1980s, Rosie Scenario. The projections from the Office of Management and Budget show the budget gap gradually shrinking as a percentage of gross domestic product, from over 6% currently to under 3% of GDP by 2036. The total publicly held U.S. debt would rise from 100% of GDP in fiscal 2025 to 103% in 2029, before easing to 94% by 2036.

OMB's projections are based on an assumed real U.S. economic growth rate of 3% annually — half-again the Federal Reserve's long-term sustainable estimate of 2%, and two-thirds greater than the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office's long-term 1.8% growth estimate.

Using the CBO's more realistic assumption, the annual deficit would rise to 7% of GDP by 2028, then ease to 6.4% in 2032 and remain there by 2036, according to the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, or CRFB. The total public debt would rise to 107% of GDP by fiscal 2028, topping the previous peak of 106% after World War II, and continue climbing to 124% of GDP by 2036 — 30 percentage points higher than the administration's projections.

As the late economist Herb Stein famously observed, if something can't go on forever, it will stop. In the case of the nation's burgeoning debt, something will have to stop it. How that happens will depend on whose oxen are gored. Spoiler alert: That may be bondholders, Social Security recipients, or just about everybody.

In terms of the current budget, the administration's ask for more money for the military is up to Congress. Many Republicans have voiced reservations about the $1.5 trillion defense request for next year, although the $200 billion for this year has a better chance (although it could be cut by as much as half), Cantrill writes.

Overall, the CRFB estimates the administration's budget plan would lift total defense funding by $3.2 trillion over 10 years. To offset this cost, the budget imposes cuts in nondefense discretionary spending by 10% in fiscal 2027. Then the budget envisages 2% annual reductions in NDD, totaling $2.5 trillion over the decade. Those cuts surely would be the subject of contentious debate in Congress.

Another point of disagreement between OMB and CBO assumptions is concerns interest rates. OMB forecasts the benchmark 10-year Treasury note yield will drop from 4.3% in 2025 to 3.4% by 2029, and stabilize at 3.3% thereafter. CBO assumes the 10-year Treasury yield will rise from 4.1% in 2026 to 4.4% in 2031-36.

Interest on the debt is a key factor in the deficit, since this expense has come to exceed defense spending. The interest tab is inevitably headed higher, the product of $2 trillion in annual deficits to be funded on top of $39 trillion of accumulated debt, and higher interest yields on new securities compared with maturing ones. The average interest rate on all interest-bearing federal debt was 3.365% as of March, compared with 4.32% on the 10-year note Friday.

Since late 2023, the Treasury has tried to limit the rise in burgeoning interest expense by issuing more short-term bills rather than intermediate- and longer-dated securities. With the three-month T-bill yield at 3.68%, that represents real savings to Uncle Sam. When this tack was taken by former Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, Scott Bessent was among several vocal critics. But his Treasury has continued leaning on short-term bills.

More drastic ideas to lower the federal government's interest burden include a so-called Mar-a-Lago Accord, floated back in late 2024 by Stephen Miran, then in the private sector, and now a Fed governor. This supposed deal would have foreign official holders of Treasuries exchange them for new, superlong obligations with low or no interest. That give-up would represent an implicit payment for the security imparted by the U.S. to the rest of the world. Given recent geopolitical events, that debt-swap idea may be even more unlikely.

Still, DoubleLine founder and CEO Jeffrey Gundlach, a noted bond investor, wouldn't dismiss the possibility of the Treasury forcing a reduction some day in the future in the interest coupons it pays bondholders. In a recent podcast, he said he instructed his government bond managers to swap their current holdings for the lowest-coupon-rate equivalents, even though the chance of such a forced swap is remote.

Social Security represents another inexorably rising expense, with the CBO forecasting its trust fund will be depleted by 2032. That would mean an average reduction in retirees' benefits of about 28% in 2033, according to CRFB estimates.

One modest proposal from the CRFB to stave off that draconian outcome: Cap Social Security benefits at $100,000 per couple, based on their normal retirement-age benefit. The relatively small fraction of top earners, who typically also have accumulated significant assets (like most Barron's readers), would be capped at six figures so that those who rely on monthly Social Security wouldn't suffer cuts.

These radical steps to rein in the deficit may be off the radar for now. A stealthier and less obvious plan would be financial repression, in which interest rates are held down below the rate of nominal growth of the economy, which consists of real growth plus inflation. That is how the post--World War II debt was pared to just 24% of GDP by 1974.

In the short term, however, the drumbeat for more defense spending only adds to the deficit burden.

Write to Randall W. Forsyth at randall.forsyth@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Moody's revises Ghana's outlook to 'positive' on improvement in domestic financing
data.description:
Credit ratings agency Moody's revised Ghana's outlook to "positive" from "stable" on Friday, citing a durable improvement in domestic financing conditions that has led to a lasting improvement to the country's debt affordability.

The agency kept Ghana's ratings at "Caa1".

data.title: Hemab Therapeutics, Clinical-Stage Coagulation Biotech, Files for NASDAQ IPO
data.description:
Hemab Therapeutics Holdings, Inc., a clinical-stage biotech developing therapies for blood coagulation disorders, filed for an initial public offering. The company plans to list on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker "COAG." Proceeds will support late-stage development of its lead assets for Glanzmann thrombasthenia, Factor VII deficiency, and Von Willebrand Disease.

Business Description

Hemab Therapeutics is a clinical-stage biotechnology company developing subcutaneous prophylactic therapies for serious bleeding disorders. Its lead asset, sutacimig (HMB-001), is a bispecific antibody designed to direct endogenous Factor VII/VIIa to activated platelets to enhance local thrombin generation at bleeding sites. Sutacimig is in Phase 1/2 development for Glanzmann thrombasthenia and in Phase 2 for Factor VII deficiency, with plans for a Phase 3 study in Glanzmann thrombasthenia.

The company’s second asset, HMB-002, is a monovalent antibody targeting the VWF CK domain to stabilize endogenous von Willebrand factor and increase Factor VIII levels, in development as a subcutaneous prophylactic option for Von Willebrand Disease. Hemab maintains global economic rights, has regulatory designations including Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, Orphan Drug, and ILAP for sutacimig, and partners with Novo Nordisk and Genmab for core platform and target IP. Operations span Cambridge, MA and Copenhagen, Denmark, with plans to commercialize in the U.S., EU, Japan, GCC and select regions.

Market Overview

  • Total addressable market: The company estimates ~10,000 patients across Glanzmann thrombasthenia and Factor VII deficiency and ~120,000 Von Willebrand Disease patients in target geographies (U.S., EU, Japan, GCC, and select regions).
  • Market growth: Shift toward prophylactic therapies is expanding the treated population as awareness and access improve.
  • Market position: Aiming to build a leading coagulation franchise with multiple clinical-stage assets and potential label expansions.
  • Key competitors: Desmopressin; plasma-derived VWF products (Humate-P, Wilate, Alphanate); recombinant VWF (Vonvendi); Star Therapeutics (VGA-039); and potential off‑label alternatives.
  • Industry trends: Growing adoption of convenient subcutaneous prophylaxis, patient-centric trial designs, and strong interest in rare disease therapies with regulatory incentives.

Operational Metrics

  • Customers: Pre-commercial; no product revenue to date
  • Locations: Cambridge, Massachusetts (HQ) and Copenhagen, Denmark
  • Geographic presence: Target launch markets include the U.S., EU, Japan, GCC countries, and select regions
  • Partnerships: Exclusive licenses with Novo Nordisk (TLT-1/Factor VII bispecific IP) and Genmab (DuoBody platform)
  • Pipeline status: Sutacimig Phase 1/2 (GT), Phase 2 (F7 deficiency), Phase 3 planned; HMB-002 Phase 1/2 (VWD); first preclinical program to clinic in H2 2026
  • Regulatory designations: FDA Fast Track and Breakthrough Therapy; FDA and EMA Orphan Drug; UK ILAP for sutacimig (Glanzmann thrombasthenia)
  • Capital raised: Approximately $346 million since inception

Financials Highlights

  • Revenue (current): No product revenue
  • Operating income: Loss from operations of $(70.1) million in 2025
  • Net income: Net loss of $(63.9) million in 2025

Management

  • Benny Sørensen, M.D., Ph.D., President and Chief Executive Officer - Over 25 years in coagulation medicine; prior roles at Codiak, Alnylam, and Baxter; led programs in hemophilia and rare bleeding disorders.
  • Mads Behrndt, Chief Financial Officer and General Manager - Former corporate finance partner at PwC with extensive transaction and growth financing experience.
  • Catherine Madigan, M.D., Chief Medical Officer - Hematology leader; previously CMO at Syndax; earlier roles at Alnylam and Biogen’s Rare Disease unit.
  • Ananthram Murthy, Ph.D., Chief Operating Officer - Former GM EMEA at argenx; prior leadership roles at Alnylam and Celgene in market access and commercialization.
  • John M. Maraganore, Ph.D., Chair of the Board - Founding CEO of Alnylam; venture and strategic advisor with deep RNAi and rare disease expertise.

IPO Structure

  • Issuer: Hemab Therapeutics Holdings, Inc.
  • Filing date: April 10, 2026
  • Proposed ticker: COAG
  • Exchange: NASDAQ
  • Lead underwriters: Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, Jefferies, Evercore ISI; lead manager: Wedbush PacGrow
  • Use of proceeds: Fund clinical development of sutacimig for Glanzmann thrombasthenia and Factor VII deficiency, HMB-002 for Von Willebrand Disease; advance discovery/preclinical programs; and for working capital and general corporate purposes.

Original SEC Filing: Hemab Therapeutics Holdings, Inc. - S-1 - Apr. 10, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Is on an 8-Day Winning Streak. Why It's Stuck in a Range. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Nvidia stock extended its winning streak Friday, closing higher for an eighth consecutive trading session, as investors carried on buying tech stocks following a key inflation report.

Shares in the chip maker climbed 2.6% to $188.75 on Friday. The S&P 500 was little changed after March inflation data came in a touch below what economists were expecting.

Nvidia has now risen for eight straight sessions through Friday's close, its longest winning streak since Nov. 14, 2023 when it rose for 10 straight trading days, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

The streak isn't as impressive as it sounds. The broader market has also racked up gains over the period, with the S&P 500 also up seven trading days in a row.

Rival chip maker Intel has also now closed higher for eight straight sessions, adding 51% in that spell. Nvidia is up just 14% over the same period.

The chip maker was the market's go-to artificial intelligence pick for years, but it has traded between $165 and $195 for months as investors wonder how long so-called hyperscalers can maintain their aggressive capital spending.

Even the longest winning streak in two and a half years hasn't enabled shares to break out of that narrow range.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Excellon Grants Stock Options and Share Units
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - April 10, 2026) - Excellon Resources Inc. (OTC Pink: EXNRF) (FSE: E4X2) ("Excellon" or the "Company") announces that the Board of Directors has approved the grant of 1,705,000 stock options, 1,700,000 performance share units and 405,000 deferred share units to directors, officers and employees of the Company pursuant to the Company's Share Incentive Plan. The stock options are exercisable at a price of $0.43 per share, expire three years from the date of grant, and vest as to one-quarter on the date of grant and as to one-quarter on each of the dates falling 6, 12 and 18 months from the date of grant.

About Excellon Resources Inc.

Excellon's vision is to realize opportunities through the acquisition and advancement of quality precious and base metal assets, leveraging an experienced management team for the benefit of its employees, communities and shareholders. The Company is focused on the potential restart of the Mallay Silver Mine in Peru. Excellon also holds a portfolio of exploration-stage projects, including the Tres Cerros Gold/Silver Exploration Property in Peru; Kilgore, an advanced gold project in Idaho; and Silver City, a high-grade epithermal silver district in Saxony, Germany, providing additional growth upside. Additional details on Excellon's properties can be found at www.excellonresources.com.

For Further Information, Please Contact:

Excellon Resources Inc.

Shawn Howarth, President & Chief Executive Officer

info@excellonresources.com

data.title: Dow, S&P 500 end lower as Middle East developments keep investors on edge
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nasdaq ends up 0.35%; S&P 500 off 0.11%, Dow off ~0.56%
  • Staples weakest S&P 500 sector; Tech leads gainers
  • Dollar, gold dip; US crude down ~2%, bitcoin up >1%
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield rises to ~4.32%

DOW, S&P 500 END LOWER AS MIDDLE EAST DEVELOPMENTS KEEP INVESTORS ON EDGE

The S&P 500 and Dow ended lower on Friday with investors on edge about developments in the Middle East as claims continued that a two-week ceasefire agreement between Iran and the United States has been violated.

Israel's continued bombardment of Lebanon is among the possible violations, while the Strait of Hormuz was kept closed by Iran.

U.S. Vice President JD Vance departed for "make-or-break" negotiations with Iran, even as Tehran insisted on measures before peace talks could take place, throwing last-minute doubt over the meetings scheduled in Pakistan.

The S&P 500 ended a seven-day streak of gains.

But all three of the major U.S. stock indexes logged their biggest weekly percentage gains since November of last year.

The Nasdaq ended higher on the day, and technology led gains among S&P 500 sectors. An index of semiconductors hit a record closing high, and closed higher for an eighth straight session.

Consumer staples led declines among S&P 500 sectors.

Here is the closing market snapshot:

data.title: U.S. Stocks Roared Back This Week, Buoyed by a Middle East Cease-Fire — WSJ
data.description:
By Vicky Ge Huang

Stocks notched their best weekly gains of the year, with investors pinning hopes on a diplomatic end to the Iran conflict and looking past a war-fueled surge in consumer prices.

The major indexes rallied in the two days following President Trump's announcement that the U.S. had reached a cease-fire with Iran, only to limp into the weekend after a government report showing inflation had ticked higher largely owing to the continuing conflict.

Friday's inflation report — the first major reading on inflation after the Iran war began — showed a 0.9% month-over-month increase and a year-over-year rise of 3.3% in consumer prices. The jump, which matched economists' expectations, was driven by skyrocketing energy prices. Gasoline prices surged 18.9% from a year earlier and fuel oil surged by 44.2%.

The energy-driven pressures might have started to ease. Oil prices plummeted after the president's late-Tuesday cease-fire declaration. Both U.S. and international oil prices logged their steepest weekly percentage slide since 2020, when the sudden stop of global travel caused oil prices to collapse. West Texas Intermediate, the U.S. oil benchmark, plunged 13.42% this week to $96.57 a barrel. Brent crude futures declined 12.68% to settle at $95.20 a barrel.

Investors are cautiously optimistic as Iran and the U.S. prepare for high-level talks in the Pakistani capital, Islamabad, on Saturday. And hopes for a permanent solution to the nearly six-week-old war propelled all three major U.S. indexes to their best weekly performance of the year, with each logging a gain of more than 3%.

Still, uncertainty over this weekend's negotiations persists. On Friday, the S&P fell 0.1%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average shed 0.6%, or 269 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq gained 0.4%, exiting a 10-day correction, or a 10% drop from its recent peak. The yield on the 10-year Treasury note, which rises when prices fall, edged higher to 4.316%.

"We've definitely seen some risk get put back on, but it does feel like people are still shying away from those risk assets meanwhile, particularly heading into the weekend with more talks coming," said Justin Wiggs, managing director of equity trading at Stifel Financial.

The cease-fire remains fragile. While investors hoped that shipping would return to normal at the Strait of Hormuz, traffic there remained a trickle. Economists caution that energy and goods prices that surged after the war could remain elevated for a long period even if the Strait of Hormuz were to fully reopen.

"We should expect, for the next couple of months, at least higher-than-normal inflation reports, because there will be a backlog of ships and it will take time for prices to come back down to where they were prior to the war," said Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer for Northlight Asset Management.

Higher gasoline prices are expected to erode the boost in discretionary income from higher tax refunds that many American households received under President Trump's tax overhaul, said Jeff Schulze, head of economic & market strategy at ClearBridge Investments.

"It's going to be a drag overall to the low- and the lower-middle income consumer by paying the higher prices at the pump," he said.

The snapshot of war-fueled inflation has forced investors to come to terms with the dwindling possibility that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates soon. As of Friday afternoon, futures traders were pricing in a 77% chance that the central bank will maintain its current benchmark rate by year-end.

In another sign of the war's impact, consumer sentiment fell to a record low in April, according to the University of Michigan.

A bright spot for the war-weary investors is corporate earnings, which kick off in earnest next week with reports from major banks including Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America. Expectations are high, with companies in the S&P 500 estimated to report a 12.6% growth rate in first-quarter earnings, according to FactSet.

Write to Vicky Ge Huang at vicky.huang@wsj.com

data.title: Top News Today: Stocks Mixed on AI Optimism, U.S.-Iran Trepidation
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

STOCKS: Stocks were mixed as trepidation ahead of U.S.-Iranian negotiations was offset by optimism about artificial intelligence.

TREASURYS: Treasury yields rose after March consumer inflation data increased in line with expectations.

FOREX: The U.S. dollar weakened slightly as core inflation remained subdued.

COMMODITIES: Oil futures fell slightly ahead of U.S.-Iranian talks in Islamabad.

HEADLINES

Inflation Soared to 3.3% in March, Driven by Higher Gasoline Costs

Consumer prices soared in March, pushed higher by skyrocketing gasoline prices.

Consumer prices were up 3.3% in March from a year earlier, the Labor Department said Friday, much hotter than February's gain of 2.4%.

Consumer Sentiment Hits Record Low, Michigan Survey Shows

Consumer sentiment fell in April to the lowest level recorded in the 70-plus-year history of the University of Michigan's survey, evidence of Americans' concerns that the Iran war will hit the domestic economy.

The survey's initial April reading came in at 47.6, versus 53.3 in March. Analysts polled by The Wall Street Journal were expecting a drop to 52. The April reading is below the previous low point of 50 recorded in June 2022, when the economy was facing searing inflation.

The initial April results are based almost entirely on interviews that took place between March 24 and April 6, before a tentative cease-fire took hold. The survey will be updated with a final April reading later this month, based on more recent responses.

Nike Switches Up Its Innovation Leadership

Nike's chief innovation officer is leaving the sneaker company after less than a year in the job, the company said, amid signs that its turnaround efforts are sputtering.

Chief Executive Elliott Hill elevated company veteran Tony Bignell to the position last June, one of Hill's first moves as CEO to regain Nike's footing in the innovation race. Now Bignell, who Nike said is leaving to pursue his own creative and philanthropic interests, will be the third innovation chief to exit from the role in less than three years.

He will be succeeded by Andy Caine, Nike vice president and creative director for sportswear, effective Sunday, the company said. Like Bignell, Caine will report to Phil McCartney, whom Hill appointed to the newly created position of chief innovation, design and product officer last year.

CoreWeave, Anthropic Form AI Cloud Agreement

CoreWeave will use its cloud infrastructure to help run Anthropic's Claude artificial-intelligence models as part of a new partnership between the two companies that gives CoreWeave another frontier AI-model customer.

CoreWeave shares were up 5.1% to $96.73 in premarket trading.

The collaboration will initially focus on a "phased infrastructure roll-out with the potential to expand over time," CoreWeave said.

Deficit Grows by $4 Billion in March. Military Spend on Iran War 'Uncertain,' Treasury Says.

The U.S. budget deficit grew slightly in March in part due to lower corporate taxes and higher tax refunds.

The Treasury Department, which manages U.S. finances, published the monthly budget statement on Friday, which showed the government in March spent $164 billion more than it collected. That deficit is $4 billion, or 2%, more than a year ago.

March and April are key months where the Treasury sees high tax-related activity. Individual and corporate refunds in March came in at $85 billion and $8 billion, respectively, 22% and 215% higher than the prior year. Meanwhile, gross corporate taxes came in at $15 billion, 28% lower from prior year.

Insurers Take Bigger Risks Than Before 2008-09 Crisis, Report Warns

Most insurance companies got through the 2008-09 financial crisis all right. A ratings firm just warned that the industry might not get through the next one unscathed.

A.M. Best published a report Friday that finds that the investment portfolios of insurers that sell annuities hold more risky debt than they did in 2007, the year before the worst downturn since the Great Depression. The ratings firm adds that annuity portfolios had a slightly smaller financial cushion in 2024-the most recent year for which data was available-than they did in 2007.

Annuity portfolios also held more investments that had been sold by companies with which they shared some affiliation (such as a common corporate owner).

Replimune Stock Tumbles After FDA Rejects Skin Cancer Therapy for Second Time

Shares of Replimune Group sank on Friday after an application for an experimental skin cancer drug was rejected by the Food and Drug Administration for the second time.

The agency declined to approve Replimune's lead investigational immunotherapy, RP1, in combination with Bristol Myers Squibb's Opdivo for the treatment of advanced melanoma.

In a letter addressed to Kari Jeschke, Replimune's senior vice president of regulatory affairs, the FDA echoed concerns from a previous communication with the biotechnology company, largely pertaining to the study design.

Iran Has Strong Cards Going Into U.S. Talks but Risks Overplaying Its Hand

ABU DHABI-Iran is heading into this weekend's negotiations with the U.S. in a triumphant mood, having tested the limits of American power and emerging from the 40-day war in control of the Strait of Hormuz and, with it, the world's energy markets.

The question now is whether Iranian leaders will overplay this critical lever at the planned meeting in Islamabad, Pakistan, with Vice President JD Vance by insisting on maximalist demands despite losing much of Iran's military and industrial base during the war. This is something that President Trump, despite all his apparent eagerness to wind down the conflict, will likely find impossible to accept.

"From Tehran's point of view, they think that they have Trump over a barrel. They think they have weaponized the world economy, have taken everything that America can throw at them, and came out standing," said William Wechsler, director of Middle East programs at the Atlantic Council and a former senior Pentagon official. "Trump blinked first. Now, the Iranians won't take a deal unless it is a deal in which Trump and Vance completely abandon U.S. national security interests in the Middle East."

TALKING POINT Wall Street Builds New Tool to Bet Against Private Credit

Large banks including JPMorgan Chase are preparing to offer a new way for investors to bet against managers of private-credit funds.

The banks are working with S&P Global to launch an index of credit-default swaps that would protect buyers against defaults by companies included in the index, called CDX Financials. Private-credit funds managed by Apollo Global Management, Ares Management and Blackstone will make up 12% of the index, which also includes insurers, regional banks and credit-card companies.

The index would rise when the market sentiment on those firms turned negative. If it gains traction, the so-called FINDX would give debt investors and traders a fast way to hedge or short what is now a more than $3 trillion industry. Private credit increasingly touches banks, insurance companies and other parts of the financial system.

"Private credit has grown fast and there's a lot of financial exposure arising in different ways so there is a real demand for this product," said Dominique Toublan, head of credit strategy at Barclays.

Banks want the index both as a product to trade and as a tool to protect against potential losses from their own loans to private-credit fund managers.

Hedge funds are keen for a way to easily make bets on a downturn in private credit. Stress has been building, as a spate of defaults and losses, combined with fears about the fate of loans to software companies, caused a stampede of individual investors asking for their money back. Some hedge funds began trying to short individual stocks and bonds issued by firms that invest in private credit, but the process was cumbersome and costly, one hedge-fund manager said.

Boaz Weinstein's Saba Capital is among the investors advocating for the creation of the new product, people familiar with the matter said. Weinstein has had a big win trading similar indexes in the past. He is also stalking private credit in other ways, including his offer in February to buy out shareholders in a fund managed by Blue Owl Capital at discounted prices.

  • Matt Wirz and Alexander Saeedy

Expected Major Events for Monday

12:30/CAN: Feb Building permits

14:00/US: Mar Existing Home Sales

23:01/UK: Mar BRC-KPMG Retail Sales Monitor

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

data.title: Energy Drinks Continue to Be a Bright Spot Among Beverages — Market Talk
data.description: Energy drinks remain a standout in the beverage category, with sales up 8.5% over the last three months in the convenience channel, outpacing a 3.3% rise for non-alcoholic beverages, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Energy drinks are finding success with their pricing and innovation, and new consumers are entering the category as they prioritize functional beverages, the analysts say. Soft drinks and beers, meanwhile, posted declines over the same period, the analysts say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Corn barge basis firm on demand from exporters
data.description:
Spot basis bids for corn shipped by barge to U.S. Gulf Coast terminals were firm on Friday because of export demand and slow farmer sales, traders said.

  • A setback in grain prices recently boosted demand from South Korean importers.

  • Exporters sold 125,640 metric tons of U.S. corn to unknown destinations, the U.S. Department of Agriculture said in its daily reporting system.

  • Farmer sales of corn and soybeans have been sluggish recently as growers wait for higher prices and focus on doing field work for the spring planting season.

  • Corn and wheat futures drifted lower at the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday.

  • CIF Gulf corn barges loaded in April traded at 94 cents over CBOT May (CK26) futures. On Thursday, April barges were bid at 90 cents over futures.

  • CIF Gulf corn barges loaded in May were bid at 91 cents over futures, after trading at 91 cents and 92 cents over futures on Thursday.

  • FOB corn offers for vessels loaded in April were steady at 99 cents over futures.

  • For soybeans, CIF Gulf barges loaded in April were bid 1 cent higher at 89 cents over CBOT May (SK26) soybean futures.

  • FOB export premiums for April soybean vessels stayed around 95 cents over futures.

  • CBOT soybeans set a two-week high as soymeal futures surged.

  • USDA reported an sale of 100,000 metric tons of U.S. soymeal to Italy. It was Italy's largest single purchase of U.S. soymeal since at least 2019, according to agency data.

  • There was talk in the market that European meal buyers may increasingly need to turn to the U.S. for supplies.

  • Traders continued to watch for Chinese demand for American soybeans at a time when U.S. suppliers face stiff competition from Brazil for sales to the top importer.

  • U.S. Trade Representative ​Jamieson Greer said the ‌United States was trying to maintain a stable relationship with China, but it would complicated matters if Beijing got involved with Iran in ​a way that was counter to U.S. ​interests.

  • Greer also said he expected U.S. President Donald Trump to have a good meeting next month with Chinese ​President Xi ​Jinping.

For displays of CIF basis, click on the codes in brackets:

U.S. CIF Gulf soybeans (GRYM)

U.S. CIF Gulf corn (GRYN)

U.S. CIF Gulf SRW wheat (GRYO)

U.S. CIF Gulf HRW wheat (GRYP)

For displays of FOB basis, please click on the following codes in brackets:

U.S. FOB Gulf corn (GRZC)

U.S. FOB Gulf soybeans (GRZD)

U.S. FOB Gulf SRW wheat (GRZE)

U.S. FOB Gulf HRW wheat (GRZF)

LINKS

U.S. barge freight

U.S. export sales estimates

U.S. grain export summary

Brazil soybean export prices (SYBS)

Brazil corn export prices (SYBX)

Argentina grain prices (GRA/ARG)

Russia grain prices (GRA/RU)

data.title: CBOT wheat ends lower on plentiful world supplies
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures closed lower on Friday for a third straight session, pressured by ample global grain supplies and competition for export business, traders said.

  • Storms crossed portions of eastern Kansas and Oklahoma on Friday, bringing beneficial rains to areas where developing U.S. winter wheat crops have struggled with drought.

  • However, moisture shortfalls were expected to persist in the western Plains, a factor that appeared to underpin K.C. (KWc1) hard red winter wheat futures.

  • CBOT May soft red winter wheat (WK26) settled down 3-1/2 cents at $5.71 per bushel after a dip to $5.67-1/2, the contract's lowest since March 4.

  • K.C. May hard red winter wheat (KWK26) ended up 1/4 cent at $5.90-3/4 a bushel.

  • Minneapolis May spring wheat (MWEK26) fell 6-3/4 cents to settle at $6.11-1/2 a bushel.

  • Traders continued to digest a monthly report released by the U.S. Department of Agriculture on Thursday, in which the agency raised its forecasts of U.S. and world wheat end-year inventories more than most analysts expected.

  • Top global wheat supplier Russia approved an additional grain export quota of 5 million metric tons which will remain in force until June 30, the Interfax news agency reported on Friday. The extra volume will be added to an earlier quota of 20 million tons.

  • Proceeds intended for general corporate purposes, including debt repayment, dividends, buybacks, capex and acquisitions.
  • Notes rank equally with other unsecured obligations, pay semi-annual interest, and are redeemable with make-whole premium before Jan 15, 2036.

data.description:
AIM ImmunoTech entered into Amendment No. 1 to its Equity Distribution Agreement with Maxim Group to broaden its at-the-market equity offering program. The change removes the prior $3 million cap on common stock sales, with shares offered under the company’s effective Form S-3 shelf. AIM is concurrently filing a prospectus supplement to increase the number of shares available. Management expects the amendment to enhance flexibility to raise capital as market conditions allow.

data.title: TSX posts third straight weekly gain as tech and resource shares rally
data.description:
Key points:
  • TSX ends up 0.7% at 33,695.76
  • For the week, the index gains 1.8%
  • Materials group advances 1.8%
  • Cogeco Communications falls 7.9% after revenue miss

By Utkarsh Hathi and Fergal Smith

Canada's main stock index rose to a five-week high on Friday as domestic data showed subdued jobs growth and investors weighed prospects of a peace deal in the Middle East, with resource and technology shares among the biggest gainers.

The Toronto Stock Exchange's S&P/TSX Composite Index ended up 218.05 points, or 0.7%, at 33,695.76, marking its highest closing level since March 4.

For the week, the index was up 1.8%, marking its third straight weekly gain, after a two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran bolstered investor sentiment globally.

U.S. Vice President JD Vance departed for "make-or-break" negotiations with Iran, even as Tehran insisted on measures before peace talks could take place, throwing last-minute doubt over the meetings scheduled in Pakistan.

"If there is a more lasting agreement over the weekend, it would yield a positive result for the equities," said Shiraz Ahmed, founder at Sartorial Wealth.

Canada's economy added 14,100 jobs in March, roughly matching expectations, after shedding 83,900 in the prior month, while the unemployment rate held steady at 6.7%.

"Given continued uncertainty over U.S. trade and with energy prices adding to company costs, hiring is likely to remain cautious in the near term," Andrew Grantham, a senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets, said in a note.

The materials group , which includes metal mining shares, rose 1.8% as copper (HGc1) prices climbed.

Energy recouped some of its declines over the previous two days, adding 1.5%, even as the price of oil settled 1.3% lower at $96.57 a barrel.

Technology was up 1.1%, with shares of electronic equipment company Celestica Inc adding 7.3%.

Heavily weighted financials (.SPTTFS) ended 0.6% higher.

Just two of the 10 major sectors lost ground, including consumer staples (.GSPTTCS). It was down 0.7%.

Cogeco Communications shares fell 7.9% after the telecommunications firm missed quarterly revenue estimates.

data.title: Jet.AI Invests $5.25 Million in Verso Certificates Tracking SpaceX Preferred Shares
data.description:

The subscription totals $5.25 million, including a 5% subscription fee of $250,000, with management and performance fees waived. The company signed on April 7, 2026 and completed the subscription on April 8, 2026. The certificates are redeemable by Verso in cash or in kind upon specified redemption events.
